questions.text: I am willing to shoulder the burden of an unpopular choice.
I would deplete my emergency fund to help a loved one avoid a crisis.
I routinely evaluate relationships by whether they support my personal growth.
I sense when someone chooses a specific word because another word would reveal too much.
I handle recurring tasks differently each time based on what the situation calls for.
I find it hard to be around conflict because my body reacts so strongly to tension.
I trust that the group will eventually resolve its own circular discussions.
I routinely reserve the best seat, the best portion, or the best option for myself.
I experience longing as a physical ache in my chest.
I believe the best use of my talents is to benefit other people.
I rarely notice when a person uses qualifiers like 'maybe' or 'sort of' to soften an opinion they feel strongly about.
I hold the view that the seriousness with which I engage with others' suffering is the most authentic measure of whether I am living according to my values.
I find that I set the emotional and strategic tone in groups I join.
I keep a detailed calendar and refer to it multiple times a day.
I feel that pursuing mastery in my chosen field is worth more than diversifying my efforts for others.
I consider my ability to command attention an important professional asset.
I find that maintaining my emotional equilibrium during painful situations is what allows me to be present and useful over extended periods.
I believe that following a process faithfully is more reliable than relying on inspiration.
I tend not to notice when someone softens a criticism to spare feelings.
I hold the view that my time belongs as much to others as it does to me.
I sense when an invitation was extended to one person but worded to include the group so it would not seem exclusive.
I hold the view that the moral weight of suffering should not be discounted because the person is distant, unfamiliar, or different from me.
I tend to feel emotions at a manageable intensity rather than at their extremes.
I believe that processing human hardship as data — rather than as personal experience — leads to more effective interventions.
I find that I do not instinctively sense who the social connectors are in a group.
I pick up whichever task feels most natural in the moment rather than mapping dependencies.
I raise concerns when collaboration gets off track but leave it to the group to set boundaries.
Even reading about an intense event can produce a strong physical reaction in me.
I believe that the moral weight of suffering is the consideration that should inform the design of institutions as much as it informs individual conscience.
I find reference materials when I need them rather than curating a collection.
I find that I do my best thinking about human problems when I can set aside the emotional weight and focus on the facts.
I ask the group's permission before speaking on its behalf.
I maintain an orderly queue of tasks rather than juggling them all at once.
I notice that I stay calm in situations where others are visibly agitated.
I find that I track whose opinions carry the most weight in a group even when no one has said so explicitly.
I tend to process emotional experiences in the background while staying functionally engaged.
